coredata.dc:title: Defect-engineered sp2 carbon as highly active catalyst and reactive fuel for combustion of ammonium perchlorate
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Rui
Li, Xiaodong
Tang, Pengfei
Yang, Bo
Xie, Lifeng
Yang, Guangcheng
coredata.dc:description: Defect engineering is a vital technique to modulate the properties of carbon nanomaterials. The unique advantage of intentionally introducing intrinsic defects and heteroatom dopants in sp2 C–C bond framework is to increase the active sites, optimize the adsorption/desorption behavior of the reaction intermediates, and alter the surface charge transfer properties of various electrochemical systems. Herein, a defects and bonding disorder regulation strategy is reported to enhance both catalytic activity and combustion reactivity of sp2 carbon toward the thermal decomposition and combustion of ammonium perchlorate (AP), which is the key reaction of modern solid propellants in aerospace field. A layered defect-rich carbon (LDC) has been prepared by inlayer carbonization of graphite-like 1,3,5-Triamino-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene (TATB) crystals. The intentionally designed LDC with rich intrinsic defects and N dopants provides abundant active sites for adsorption of AP decomposition intermediates, which facilitates efficient charge transfer in rate controlling steps. Moreover, the LDC with high bond disorder can react with AP as fuel with high reaction kinetics. As compared to other carbon materials with different bonding disorder and defects, this highly reactive catalyst greatly enhances the combustion performance of AP/LDC composites in terms of more complete chemical energy release (increased combustion pressure) and enhanced reaction kinetics (increased burning rate). The defect engineering provides an effective way to activate the inert sp2 carbon materials to efficient combustion catalysts and highly reactive fuels for AP based solid propellants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Defect engineering
Carbon
Combustion
Ammonium perchlorate
Solid propellants

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Combustion pressure test in closed bomb

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11802276, 11702268, 11702264, 11772307, 21703217) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparison of the ΔH and decrease of HTD of the reported catalysts towards thermal decomposition of AP. Metal oxides favor decreasing the HTD but suffer from low ΔH. Regular carbons have poor decrease value of HTD but with higher ΔH. The modified carbons have the combined advantages of high decrease value of HTD and high ΔH, indicating that tailoring the structure of carbon materials is an effective route to develop non-metal combustion catalysts with high catalytic activity and energy density. The corresponding data and references are shown in Table S1, Supporting Information. Abbreviation: ZnO HSP, ZnO hollow microspheres; NiO NP, NiO nanoparticle; ZnO SS, ZnO superstructure; FGO, functionalized graphene oxide; 3D HOPC, 3D hierarchically ordered porous carbon; NGO, nitrated GO.

(a) Protocol of synthetizing the LDC by in-layer carbonization of the graphite-like TATB crystals. SEM images show the accordion-like (b) TATB crystal and (c) LDC-750. Inset in Fig. 2c shows the TEM image of LDC-750. (d) Carbon K-edge electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) spectra of LDC-750, n-Carbon and graphite. (e) Raman scattering spectra of LDC-750, n-Carbon and graphite. (f) Active energy and sp2 content of LDC-750, n-Carbon and graphite as a function of I D/I G ratio. Insets show the HRTEM images of LDC-750, n-Carbon and graphite. (g) Comparison of impact and friction sensitivities of nano AP, AP/LDC-750 and typical oxidants applied in SP composite.

(a) SEM image of AP/LDC-750 composite. (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of LDC-750 and AP/LDC-750. Inset shows the corresponding pore size distribution. (c) SEM image of typical AP/LDC-750 composite and corresponding C, N and Cl elemental mapping. (d) XRD patterns of LDC-750, AP and AP/LDC-750 composite.

(a) Experimental setup of the laser ignition and combustion test for AP/LDC composites. (b-d) Combustion process of samples by laser ignition, (b) AP/Graphite, (c) AP/n-Carbon, (d) AP/LDC-750. (e) The luminous intensity-time trace of the flame obtained from integrating the emission spectra at different time. (f) Comparison of the maximum flame height of different AP composites.

(a) Experimental setup of the combustion pressure test for AP/LDC composites. (b) Pressure–time curves of AP composites with different sp2 carbon additives. Maximum pressure (c) and average pressurization rate (d) extracted from the pressure–time curves shown in Fig. 5(b).

head.ce:title: Defect-engineered sp2 carbon as highly active catalyst and reactive fuel for combustion of ammonium perchlorate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemical Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Defect engineering is a vital technique to modulate the properties of carbon nanomaterials. The unique advantage of intentionally introducing intrinsic defects and heteroatom dopants in sp2 C–C bond framework is to increase the active sites, optimize the adsorption/desorption behavior of the reaction intermediates, and alter the surface charge transfer properties of various electrochemical systems. Herein, a defects and bonding disorder regulation strategy is reported to enhance both catalytic activity and combustion reactivity of sp2 carbon toward the thermal decomposition and combustion of ammonium perchlorate (AP), which is the key reaction of modern solid propellants in aerospace field. A layered defect-rich carbon (LDC) has been prepared by inlayer carbonization of graphite-like 1,3,5-Triamino-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene (TATB) crystals. The intentionally designed LDC with rich intrinsic defects and N dopants provides abundant active sites for adsorption of AP decomposition intermediates, which facilitates efficient charge transfer in rate controlling steps. Moreover, the LDC with high bond disorder can react with AP as fuel with high reaction kinetics. As compared to other carbon materials with different bonding disorder and defects, this highly reactive catalyst greatly enhances the combustion performance of AP/LDC composites in terms of more complete chemical energy release (increased combustion pressure) and enhanced reaction kinetics (increased burning rate). The defect engineering provides an effective way to activate the inert sp2 carbon materials to efficient combustion catalysts and highly reactive fuels for AP based solid propellants.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid propellant (SP) is the matter base and propulsion energy source for realizing the remote delivery of rocket/missile [1,2]. The combustion performance of SP, such as burning rate (u), combustion pressure and enthalpy change (ΔH), is an important factor affecting the ballistic performance and work ability of the rocket/missile [3]. Ammonium perchlorate (AP), the most commonly used oxidizers, accounts for 60–80% of the total weight of modern SP propellants [4]. Its energy release behavior, including thermal decomposition temperature, thermal decomposition rate and ΔH, has a decisive effect on the combustion performance of SP. Thus, combustion catalysts (or ballistic modifier) are applied to improve the combustion performance of SP via tailoring the energy release behavior of AP [1,5–8].

A variety of materials, such as metal oxides [7,9,10], carbon materials [11–13], metal oxides/carbon materials hybrids [14–16], have been developed as combustion catalysts. Metal oxides, including single metal oxides and polymetallic oxides, are the benchmark catalysts since the unique adsorption and acid-base properties of the metal oxides surfaces are beneficial to catalyzing thermal decomposition of AP [10]. With a small amount of metal oxides, usually 2% to 5% [9,17], the high thermal decomposition (HTD) temperature of AP can be reduced by 40–160 °C, much superior to the reduction of carbon materials (65–84 °C), Fig. 1 . However, due to the non-energetic feature, the addition of metal oxides will reduce the total energy of SP [13]. Carbon-based catalysts offer certain advantages over the metal-based catalysts owing to their light-weight and low-cost. At the same time, it is also a fuel with high combustion enthalpy and a large amount of gas production. Unfortunately, the conventional carbon materials, such as graphene, carbon black, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) and graphene oxide (GO), have poor catalytic activity for thermal decomposition of AP [12,18–20]. To overcome the disadvantage of carbon-based catalysts, recent work has focused on modification their structure and/or physicochemical properties [11,13,18,21]. For instance, the 3D hierarchically ordered porous carbon (3D-HOPC) exhibits excellent catalytic activity for the thermal decomposition of AP with reduction of HTD by 125.5 °C and increase of ΔH from 371 to 3765 J/g [11]. The nitrated GO (NGO) reduces the HTD of AP by 106 °C and increases the ΔH from 875 to 3236 J g−1 [18]. These works demonstrate that reasonable architecture design or chemical modification is an efficient method to increase the catalytic activity of carbon nanomaterials. However, the burning rate of the present carbon materials is too low to serve as fuels in SP applications owing to their slow reaction kinetics [12,22,23]. Therefore, developing carbon material with high catalytic activity and high combustion reactivity is still challenging.

The sp2 carbon is composed of hexagonal arranged sp2-carbon atoms. The strong sp2 C–C bond is the source of high enthalpy of combustion, and also the origin of its thermodynamic stability. Graphite is the most thermodynamically stable carbon allotrope under ambient conditions owing to its full sp2-bonded structure [24,25]. Its one single layer allotrope, the graphene, inherits the stability of graphite. In contrast, the single walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) with distorted sp2 hybrid state have relatively high reactivity, which allows SWNTs to be ignited in air by photoflash [26]. It is also found that defects can drastically modify the electronic properties and thermodynamic stability of the carbon nanomaterials [27–29]. For example, the amorphous carbon with low sp2 bond and high bond disorder is easier to be removed in the purification of SWNTs by oxidation or dissolution than graphite sheet [30]. The pristine multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWNT) is more thermally stable towards oxidative destruction than the HNO3-H2SO4 treated one [31]. Therefore, reducing the sp2 content or increasing the bonding disorder is an effective way to promote the reactivity of sp2 carbon materials.

Inspired by the above-mentioned phenomena, we propose a defects and bond disorder regulation strategy to enhance both catalytic activity and combustion reactivity of the sp2 carbon to AP. A layered defect-rich carbon (LDC) with low sp2 content and high bond disorder was prepared by one-step in-layer carbonization of the layered 1,3,5-Triamino-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene (TATB) crystals. TATB is a symmetric planar molecule, which is composed of a benzene ring with three nitro (–NO2) and three amine (–NH2) groups. TATB molecules are stacked into a graphitic-like layered structure by intramolecular hydrogen bonds between –NO2 and –NH2. The unique structure and thermal stability of TATB make it break through the scope of explosives and serves as carbon/nitrogen source in preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials [32–36]. Due to its unique physical and chemical properties, TATB can also be extended to construct defect-rich carbon materials for the following considerations: 1) The layered TATB molecules facilitate the formation of sp2 carbon framework by graphitization within layers, resulting in layered stacking of carbon sheets [37]; 2) the C–NO2 bond cleavage, amino dehydrogenation and ring cleavage reactions during the decomposition of TATB [37,38] result in distortion and merging of the nearby carbon rings, which benefits formation of sp2 carbon sheets with rich defects; 3) the considerable gas products could lead to the expansion of the layered carbon structure and create a large number of pores in the material; 4) high nitrogen doping increases the type and number of defects, which is expected to improve the reactivity and catalytic activity of LDC. The resultant LDC shows excellent catalytic activity for thermal decomposition of AP. Furthermore, the advancement of catalytic performance of our LDC for real combustion of AP has also been confirmed by laser ignited combustion test and closed bomb test.

Firstly, 0.5 g TATB powder was evenly spread on the bottom of a porcelain boat. The porcelain boat was then placed in the middle of a horizontal tubular quartz furnace. Under the protection of N2/H2 mixture (volume ratio of 95:5), LDC-T powders were obtained by raising the temperature to the specified temperature at the rate of 3 °C/min and holding for 1 h. Here, the T represents the specified temperature: 650, 750, 850 and 1000 °C.

Ammonium Perchlorate/carbon composites for thermal analysis and combustion were prepared by solvent evaporation method. Typically, 100 mg AP was dissolved in acetone solution, then 25 mg carbon material was added and ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min. At last, the suspension was stirred at 60 °C until the solvent evaporated. The final product was dried at 60 °C in vacuum for 2 h.

The combustion performance of AP/LDC composites at room temperature (20 to 30 °C) was tested in open environment. Prior to the laser ignition, 20 mg AP/LDC composite was poured into a shallow pit (ϕ7 × 1 mm) of an aluminum sample table and flattened with a glass sheet to form a small cylindrical pellet of ϕ7 × 1 mm in size. The AP/LDC pellet was ignited using a 1064 nm semiconductor laser (MFSC-10, Maxphotonics Co., Ltd, China). The laser power and pulse width were set to 10 W and 118 ms, respectively. The beam diameter was 2.0 mm. The combustion process was recorded by a high-speed camera (Photron VE0410L) at 1000 frames per second.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combustion pressure test in closed bomb
ce:section.ce:para: For combustion pressure test, the AP based composites with different sp2 carbon materials were put on the bottom of a bomb, which is cylindrical with an inner diameter of 7.5 cm and a height of 7.5 cm. The mass of the sample was fixed at 100 ± 5 mg for each test. Ni–Cr alloy ignition wire in close contact with the composite was used as ignitor. The power source for ignition was 30 V, 5A. A fast response pressure sensor (Model 113B21, PCB Piezotronics) was used to measure the combustion pressure. The pressure change was amplified and converted into electrical signal by the signal conditioner (Model 482A20, PCB Piezotronics) and the data acquisition (Model PCI-1112). Each sample was measured three times.

The morphological and structural properties of the samples were characterized by field-emission SEM (Zeiss Sigma HD), TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 F20) equipped with EDS spectroscopy (Oxford65), XRD (X’Pert Pro MPD with Cu Kα source), Raman (Renishaw InVia micro-Raman Microscope), and XPS (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi). The electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) was recorded on a FEI Themis Z scanning/transmission electron microscope (STEM) with a probe corrector at 300 kV. All samples were analyzed by thermogravimetry–differential scanning calorimetry (TG-DSC 1LF, Mettler-Toledo). Argon and oxygen were used as reaction gases for thermal analysis of AP composites and sp2 carbon materials, respectively. The luminescence of AP composites during the combustion process was recorded by an Ocean FX-UV–VIS Spectrometer (200–850 nm) with integral time of 1 ms. The mechanical sensitivity was determined by standard Bundesanstalt-für-Materialforschung (BAM) method [39].

Fig. 2 a shows our synthesis protocol for generating LDC using in-layer carbonization of the graphite-like TATB crystals. Initial TATB crystals, consisting of layers of intermolecular hydrogen bonded TATB molecules, show an accordion-like structure due to large interlayer spacing and weak interlayer interaction, Fig. 2b. When heated in a protective atmosphere (such as N2/H2), the initial reactions, including C–NO2 bond cleavage and amino dehydrogenation [38] within or between TATB molecules, produce N-doped carbon clusters. The carbon clusters are then enlarged to carbon sheets [37]. The subsequent molecular ring fission of TATB prevents the long-range graphitization and results in the formation of carbon sheets with abundant defects. The N doping should result from carbonization of TATB residual fragments containing -C-N and the gas phase N doping by N-containing gas phase intermediates, such as the NH3 and HCN, which are typical products observed in previous works [37,38,40]. During the carbonization reaction, a large amount of gaseous products, such as H, H2, N2, H2O, NO, O2, C3H2, and C3H3 [33] are produced in the process of C–NO2 bond cleavage, amino dehydrogenation, hydrogen transfer, NO2 reduction, carbon oxidation and rupture of the molecular ring [38,40]. Because of the large interlayer spacing of TATB crystal and the expansion effect induced by the gas products, the carbonization tends to occur in layers. Moreover, the expansion caused by gas will also open the interspace and create pores in the interlayer, thus forming a porous accordion-like structure in a wide carbonization temperature range of 650 °C ~ 1000 °C, as shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S1. The layered structure of LDC could be confirmed by TEM image shown in Fig. 2c. To perform TEM characterization, the typical LDC synthetized at 750 °C (LDC-750) was ultrasonically dispersed in alcohol for 1 min. It is found that the LDC-750 can partially split into congeries of small carbon sheet even under mild dispersion conditions, indicating the week interlayer interaction of the LDC-750 due to its rich defects and high bond disorder.

The defect and bond disorder information of the resultant LDC samples were characterized by electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), Raman scattering and X-ray photoelectric spectroscopy (XPS). For comparison, commercial nano sized carbon (n-Carbon) and graphite were also studied. As shown in Fig. 2d and Fig. S2, the low percentage of sp2 bonding in LDC is evident from the low ratio of π* bonding to π* + σ* bonding. Taking graphite as reference (100%), the sp2 bonding is 60.8% and 90.6% in LDC-750 and n-Carbon, respectively. The low sp2 bond in LDC-750 is due to the unique molecule and crystal structure of TATB, which facilitates local carbonization, forming sp2 carbon with multiple types of defects, including sp3 carbon bond, N doping, vacancy, non-hexagonal carbon ring and so on. Although LDC has abundant defects, the sp2 bond is still dominant in the carbon framework of LDC, allowing inheriting of the high combustion enthalpy of sp2 carbon materials. The rich defect and high bond disorder of LDC are further verified by the high overlap of D band and G band in Raman scattering spectra (Fig. 2e and Fig. S3). The I D/I G ratios of LDC-750 and LDC-850 are 3.41 and 3.29 respectively, which are much larger than 2.17 of nano carbon (n-Carbon) and 0.349 of graphite. XPS results reveal the presence of C, N and slight O in LDC (Fig. S4 and Table S2). The N dopant in LDC-650 is as high as 24.41%, which gradually decreases to 13.22%, 8.12% and 4.47% for LDC-750, LDC-850 and LDC-1000, respectively. Only C and slight O in n-Carbon and graphite were observed. The C 1 s core-level spectrum shown in Fig. S4b can be deconvoluted into three peaks located at ~284.8 eV, ~285.7 eV and ~287.2 eV, which was assigned to C = C/C − C, C = N, and C − O/C − N, respectively. The peaks corresponding to C = C/C − C become narrower with the increase of carbonization temperature owing to the improvement of graphitization. Detailed XPS line shape analysis of the N 1 s peak reveals that the N doping consists of four types of nitrogen, including graphitic (N-Q), pyridinic (N-6), pyrrole (N-5) and N-X. The small N-X peak possibly corresponds to the forms similar to N-oxide of pyridine or other oxidized nitrogen functionalities after exposure to air [41]. In all the LDC samples, graphitic/pyridinic N are the main components. It is reported that carbon defects can serve as Lewis base sites [42]. Since little N-X was existed in all the samples (Table S2), the graphitic/pyridinic N doping could thus enhance the Lewis basicity [43]. The resulting Lewis base sites should be beneficial to the adsorption and diffusion of HClO4 (the reaction intermediate of AP) via Lewis acid-base interaction [44,45], thus enhancing the electron transfer in the decomposition of HClO4. Moreover, N doping can also enhance the proton transfer between NH4 + and ClO4 - on the basis of the report that the N atoms in carbon framework are proton-donor/acceptor sites in chemical reactions [46,47]. Thus, the high N doping in LDC could not only promote the reactivity of sp2 carbon framework owing to the low thermodynamic stability of N-5 and N-6 dopants [48], but also enhance the charge transfer in the decomposition process of AP.

To systematically investigate the effect of defects and bonding disorder on the catalytic and reactive behavior of AP/carbon composites, four typical sp2 carbon materials, including graphene oxide (GO), activated carbon (AC), n-Carbon and graphite, were considered for comparison. Since the surface oxygen functional groups of GO (e.g., epoxide, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups) lead to a poor or even negative effect on its catalytic performance [13,18], GO can not serve as an active catalyst and reactive fuel for combustion of AP based composites, which is confirmed by the poor combustion performance of AP/GO composite, as shown in Fig. S5. The AC has a large specific surface area, but its defect level is between graphite and n-Carbon (Fig. 2e and Fig. S6), which is insufficient to achieve a self-sustaining combustion, as shown in Fig. S5b. Therefore, we choose LDC, n-Carbon, and graphite with different defects level and bonding disorder as representative materials for comparison study.

Firstly, the reactivity of the three types of sp2 carbon materials, namely the graphite with long-range order (left inset, Fig. 2f), the n-Carbon with short-range order (middle inset, Fig. 2f), and the LDC-750 with rich defects and high disorder (right inset, Fig. 2f), were evaluated by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis. As shown in Fig. S7a, the decomposition of LDC-750 in O2 atmosphere exhibits a broader exothermic peak with lower onset temperature as compared to that of n-Carbon and graphite, giving an activation energy (E a) of 123. 6 kJ g−1, much lower than that of 215.1 kJ g−1 for n-Carbon and 247.3 kJ g−1 for graphite (Fig. S7b and S7c). The lower onset temperature and E a value of LDC-750 with higher defects and bond disorder indicate that the reactivity of the sp2 carbons is greatly promoted by defect engineering (Fig. 2f). The LDC with high reactivity is expected to be a high-performance combustion catalyst, which can greatly promote both thermal decomposition and real combustion of the oxidizer used in SP, such as AP in this work.

Safety is also an important concern of modern propellants. In order to evaluate the influence of the reduction of E a on the safety of AP/LDC-750 composite, mechanical sensitivity was tested according to the standard Bundesanstalt-für-Materialforschung (BAM) method [39]. It is found that the impact and friction sensitivity are positively correlated with the defect level and reactivity of carbon materials, namely the sensitivity ranks in order of AP/LDC-750 > AP/n-Carbon > AP/graphite, as shown in Table S3, Supporting Information. Even so, the impact sensitivity of AP/LDC-750 (17 J) is still lower than that of nano AP (13 J, see the morphological information in Fig. S8) used in this work and the regular AP (10 J) in previous report [6], as shown in Fig. 2g and Table S3. The impact energy of AP/LDC-750 is also superior to the commonly used energetic combustion additives in SP, such as RDX (7.5 J) [49], HMX (8 J) [6] and CL-20 (2 J) [50]. Moreover, the friction sensitivity of AP/LDC-750 is 180 N, which is lower than that of nano AP (greater than360 N), but much larger than that of regular AP (28 N) [6], RDX (120 N) [49], HMX (84 N) [6] and CL-20 (108 N) [50]. The sensitivity results show that AP/LDC-750 composite has the combined advantages of high reactivity and high safety.

Fig. 3 shows the morphological and structural characteristics of AP/LDC-750 composite. Compared with the SEM of LDC-750 shown in Fig. 2c, AP/LDC-750 shows much higher roughness with AP loading. Although the loading content of AP is as large as 80% to the total weight of the composite, the layered structure of LDC-750 still remains after loading AP, indicating that AP nanoparticles should be distributed in the porous LDC-750. As an evidence, the BET specific surface area and pore volume of LDC-750 are as large as 329 m2/g and 0.93 cm3/g, indicating the existence of abundant inner space in LDC-750 due to unique thermal decomposition property of TATB. The abundant inner spaces in porous LDC-750 are expected to form AP/carbon composite with large contact area. As expected, the BET surface area and pore volume decrease sharply to 15 m2/g and 0.13 cm3/g after AP loading, as shown in Fig. 3b and Table S4. Moreover, the sharp peak of 3.4 nm ~ 3.5 nm in the pore size distribution curve disappears after AP loading, further indicating that AP nanoparticles are fully filled in the inner hole of LDC-750 and are in close contact with the inner surface of LDC-750, which is a desired feature of high efficiency catalysis. The EDS elemental mappings of the AP/LDC-750 composite clearly identify that C, N and Cl are the main elementals and have uniform distribution (Fig. 3c), indicating homogeneous distribution of AP nanoparticles in the matrix of porous LDC-750. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis reveals that AP nanoparticles confined in LDC-750 have good crystallinity (Fig. 3d). The average size of AP nanoparticles in LDC-750 is ~ 76.2 nm according the Scherrer equation, comparable to that of the pristine AP nanoparticles (~87.0 nm) used in this work.

To evaluate the effect of defects and bonding disorder on the combustion performance of AP/carbon composites, a laboratory-scale combustion test similar to the previous report [51] was carried out. The experimental setup of laser ignition and combustion test of AP/LDC composites is shown in Fig. 4 a. For comparison, AP/n-Carbon and AP/graphite were also tested. In a typical experiment, 20 mg of the AP composite was filled in a shallow pit in the center of an aluminum plate, and then pressed into a loosely packed pellet with the same size as the pit. A focused 1064 nm laser with a spot size of 2 mm was used for ignition. The reaction process of AP composites, including the ignition event, establishment of the luminous flame and steady-state combustion, was captured by a high-speed camera and an optical fiber spectrometer. Fig. 4b to d show the optical images of the representative samples of AP/graphite, AP/n-Carbon and AP/LDC-750 composite exposed to laser irradiation (118 ms duration, 2 mm spot size, 318.5 W/cm2). Under the laser ignition condition we used, AP/graphite can not be ignited, and only purple glow along the laser beam can be observed for AP/graphite (Fig. 4b), which is the result of the interaction between laser and gasification products of AP/graphite during laser irradiation. The failure in ignition of AP/graphite indicates low activity of the graphite for both thermal decomposition and combustion of AP due to its high E a (Fig. 2f and Fig. S7).

As expected, the AP composites with short-range ordered n-Carbon and highly defective and disordered LDC-T (T = 750, 850 and 1000 °C) as catalysts can be ignited to establish steady-state combustion, as shown in Fig. 4c, d and Fig. S9. The visual ‘first light’ was occurred at 6 ms for AP/n-Carbon, and then the flame grew slowly and snapped back rapidly after the laser was turned off at 118 ms. The burning time (the time from ‘first light’ to flame extinguishing) of AP/n-Carbon was 685 ± 15 ms. For AP/LDC composites, all samples could be ignited and establish steady-state combustion except for AP/LDC-650. Moreover, the AP/LDC composites show shortened burning time as compared to that of AP/n-Carbon: 340 ± 18 ms for AP/LDC-750, 353 ± 12 ms for AP/LDC-850 and 545 ± 17 ms for AP/LDC-1000. It is noteworthy that the snapping back of the flame after laser off was absent in AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850 composites. This result indicates that the reaction between AP and LDC with rich defects and bond disorder (AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850) can provide enough heat energy to feed back to the system, and thus triggers the subsequent reactions at neighboring sites. For the less defective AP/LDC-1000, the burning time was prolonged (545 ± 17 ms), and the flame snapping back phenomenon after laser off was similar to that of AP/n-Carbon.

Time resolved emission spectroscopy gives more precise information for the laser ignition and combustion process. The emission spectra at the highest luminous intensity are show in Fig. S10. The four samples with different sp2 carbon materials show identical emission spectra but with different luminous intensity. The luminous intensity-time trace of the flame was obtained by integrating each emission spectrum and plotted as a function of time, as shown in Fig. 4e. The luminous intensity of AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850 showed sharp increase with a rise time of ~ 6 ms, followed by a short period of plateau (~31 ms for AP/LDC-750 and ~ 37 ms for AP/LDC-850), and then increases sharply to form another higher plateau corresponding to the steady-state combustion. The snapping back of the flame for AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850 is not observed, which is consistent with the result from high-speed camera. As for AP/LDC-1000 and AP/n-Carbon, the initial increase time of luminous intensity is ~ 6 ms, but the luminous intensity is only 14–37% of AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850. The first luminous plateau lasts until the end of the laser irradiation. Moreover, the sharp decrease of luminous intensity is observed for AP/LDC-1000 and AP/n-Carbon, followed by slowly increasing to the peak values within 196 ms and 360 ms, respectively. The obvious snapping back and slow establishment of steady-state combustion suggest the slow reaction kinetics between n-Carbon and AP. Fig. 4f compares the maximum laminar jet flame heights of the four composites. According to the picture of diffusion flame, the laminar jet flame height (H f) is proportional to the volumetric fuel flow rate (Q f) [52,53]. Thus, the higher H f of AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850 suggests the higher pyrolysis reaction kinetics between AP and LDC [1,2]. Due to the high increase in activity of the LDC, especially LDC-750, AP/LDC composites show great improvement in combustion, in terms of short time for establishment of the steady-state combustion, strong flame intensity, high H f, and fast burning rate.

The advances of the LDC were also evaluated by a dynamic pressure–time trace measured in closed bomb with a constant volume (Fig. 5 a), which is commonly used to evaluate the combustion progress of nano energetic materials [54–56]. The typical temporal pressure traces are illustrated in Fig. 5b, from which two parameters, the peak pressure and pressurization rate, are extracted to evaluate the combustion of AP composites with different sp2 carbon materials. The combustion pressure achieved by AP/LDC composites is in a range of 217 to 266 kPa with high pressurization rate of 1.31–2.25 kPa ms−1 (Fig. 5c and d). These values outperform the AP/n-Carbon and AP/graphite composites. For LDC with different carbonization temperatures, AP/LDC-750 has the highest peak pressure of 266 kPa and the fastest pressurization rate of 2.25 kPa ms−1, indicating the high reactivity of LDC facilitates more total heat release and gas generation during combustion [54].

To reveal the source of the advances of LDC, thermal analysis was carried out on AP composites with different sp2 carbon materials. The DSC curve of AP nanoparticles contains three peaks (Fig. 6 a), one endothermic peak corresponding to crystal transformation of AP (245.1 °C) and two exothermic peaks characteristic of low-temperature decomposition (LTD, 332.7 °C) and high-temperature decomposition (HTD, 437.8 °C) [5,57]. So far, the thermal decomposition mechanism of AP is still unclear [5]. One possible mechanism can be summarized as follows:

First, a molecular complex [NH3-H-ClO4] is formed at low temperature (300–330 °C) due to local electron transfer from anion to cation [58], then it decomposes into NH3 and HClO4 with proton transfer from ammonium ion to perchlorate ion [59].

Second, the resultant NH3 and/or HClO4 are adsorbed on the surface of the residual perchlorate crystals, preventing their further reaction in the adsorbed layer or desorbing and interacting in the gas phase, which is the reason for the cessation of the reaction [5].

Third, when the temperature increases to 450 to 480 °C, complete decomposition of the intermediates (NH3 and HClO4) into gas products occurs with the generation of super-oxide radical (O2–) from decomposition of HClO4 as controlling step [60,61], which is the source of the HTD process.

Since the electron transfer in controlling step of HTD occurs locally, the smaller the distance between the ions, the higher the reaction probability of controlling step [5]. Thus, the ‘catalysts’, serving as both efficient electron acceptor and adsorption site for the reaction intermediates, are added to accelerate the electron transfer from O2 to O2 –. Nano carbons with high surface area and high electron conductivity are such ‘catalysts’ for the thermal decomposition of AP [11–13,18,21,62].

In our case of LDC, the abundant defects, such as the intrinsic carbon defects and N induced defects, favor the adsorption of HClO4 via Lewis acid-base interaction [44,45,63,64], which significantly narrows the space distance between the intermediates and realizes efficient charge transfer reaction in sequential steps. As a result, the HTD peak of AP/LDC moves to the lower temperature. The higher the carbonization temperature applied for preparing LDC, the lower the peak temperature of the HTD peak. According to the above-mentioned electron transfer mechanism, the carbonization temperature dependent variation of HTD should be due to the increase of graphitization degree with the increase of carbonization temperature, and thus the increase of electronic conductivity. When the carbonization temperature is higher than 850 °C, the two peaks merge into one peak. The catalytic property is comparable or superior to that of the n-Carbon in this work and other carbon materials in previous work, as shown in Table S1.

However, it is important to note that the catalytic performance of LDC toward thermal decomposition of AP is not consistent with their combustion behavior. Obviously, LDC-750 with poor catalytic performance exhibits the best combustion performance. This should be because carbon materials do not simply play a catalytic role in the combustion and decomposition of AP. They also react with AP as fuels, especially for our highly reactive LDC materials [11,21]. As an evidence, except for the typical LTD and HTD peaks observed in AP and AP/n-Carbon, additional exothermic peak can be observed in the DSC curves of AP/LDC-650, AP/LDC-750 and AP/LDC-850 after deconvolution by iterative least-squares curve fitting, as shown in Fig. 6a. Owing to their reaction as fuels, the heat release of AP/LDC-650 (2598 kJ g−1), AP/LDC-750 (2780 kJ g−1), and AP/LDC-850 (2637 kJ g−1) outperforms other sp2 carbon materials with lower reactivity, such as AP/LDC-1000 (2476 kJ g−1), AP/n-Carbon (2566 kJ g−1), and AP/graphite (538 kJ g−1), Fig. 6b.

To develop better combustion catalysts for AP based propellants, the thermal decomposition catalytic performance of AP is commonly investigated [5,65]. However, in this work, no rule can be found between combustion parameters (peak pressure and burning time) and thermal decomposition parameters (such as the peak temperature of HTD), Fig. 6c. Since the carbon materials serve as both catalysts and fuels in combustion of AP composites, the kinetic parameters, such as E a, should be a better parameter to evaluate the combustion performance. The E a of AP composites was calculated according to the Kissinger’ method (Fig. 6d and Table S5). A rough linear relationship can be found between the combustion parameters and E a (Fig. 6e). The AP/LDC-750 with the lowest E a value shows best combustion performances, including the highest peak pressure and pressurization rate in closed bomb test, the shortest burning time and strongest luminous intensity. These results indicate that our strategy of enhancing the reactivity of sp2 carbon by defect engineering can greatly promote the real combustion performance of AP/carbon composites. The reactive LDC could serve as both catalyst and fuel for combustion of AP/LDC composites.

The effect of defects and bonding disorder in sp2 carbon materials on catalytic thermal decomposition and combustion of AP were experimentally investigated. The layered defect-rich carbon (LDC) has been prepared by an intentionally designed in-layer carbonization method using graphitic-like TATB crystal as precursor. The LDC materials have abundant intrinsic defects and N dopants as well as the high bond disorder. Typically, the combustion performances of AP/sp2 carbon materials composites are promoted by increasing the number of defects and bonding disorder of sp2 carbon materials. The order of burning rate and combustion pressure is found to be: LDC (disorder) > n-Carbon (short-range order) > graphite (long-range order). The rich defects in LDC materials provide abundant active sites for adsorption of the decomposition intermediates of AP and enable efficient charge transfer in the rate controlling step. Moreover, the high bond disorder in LDC greatly lowers the active energy, rendering the LDC react with AP as a fuel with high reaction kinetics. The enhanced effect of LDC can be attributed to the coupling of high catalytic activity, high combustion reactivity, increased heat release and gas generation. Therefore, the defective LDC with high reactivity can effectively advance the application of sp2 carbon materials as non-metal combustion catalysts with fascinating advantages of increased burning rate, reduced non-energetic mass, increased gas generation and energy density.
